heuristics.rule: Apply for OPT exactly 90 days before your program end date — not a day later.
heuristics.condition: When you are an F-1 student approaching graduation and planning to work in the US
heuristics.action: Submit your OPT application (Form I-765) to USCIS as close to 90 days before your program end date as possible
heuristics.because: USCIS processing takes 2–5 months; applying late risks a gap in work authorization that can cost you a job offer or trigger unemployment day accumulation

heuristics.rule: After your DSO recommends OPT in SEVIS, you have exactly 30 days to file Form I-765 — treat this as a hard deadline with zero flexibility.
heuristics.condition: When your Designated School Official has recommended OPT in the SEVIS system
heuristics.action: Submit Form I-765 to USCIS within 30 days of the DSO SEVIS recommendation
heuristics.because: Missing this 30-day window results in automatic denial — there is no grace period or appeal process for this specific deadline

heuristics.rule: Before accepting any job offer for STEM OPT, verify E-Verify enrollment — not after signing.
heuristics.condition: When you hold a STEM degree and are negotiating a job offer during or leading into your STEM OPT extension period
heuristics.action: Confirm the employer is enrolled in E-Verify before accepting the offer, not after
heuristics.because: A non-E-Verify employer legally cannot employ you on STEM OPT; accepting the offer first creates pressure to join anyway, which violates your immigration status

heuristics.rule: If your H-1B petition is filed and pending when OPT expires, do not file any separate cap-gap paperwork — it is automatic.
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student's OPT expiration date falls before October 1 and an H-1B petition has been filed on their behalf
heuristics.action: Verify with employer that the H-1B petition has been filed, then continue working — no separate USCIS filing is needed for cap-gap
heuristics.because: Cap-gap extension of F-1 status and work authorization is automatic by regulation; filing unnecessary documents wastes money and can confuse the record

heuristics.rule: Start tracking unemployment days from Day 1 of your OPT period — not from when you first notice you are approaching the limit.
heuristics.action: Create a personal log of all unemployed days starting from the OPT start date on your EAD card
heuristics.because: The 90-day limit (regular OPT) and cumulative 150-day limit (STEM OPT) are strictly enforced; USCIS does not send warnings, and exceeding the limit terminates F-1 status automatically

heuristics.rule: When changing employers during STEM OPT, report the change to your DSO within 10 days and submit a new Form I-983 — do not wait until the next check-in.
heuristics.condition: When a student on STEM OPT accepts a new job or leaves a previous employer
heuristics.action: Notify DSO within 10 days and prepare a new I-983 Training Plan with the new employer before starting work
heuristics.because: Failure to report within 10 days is a SEVIS compliance violation; working without a valid I-983 on file exposes the student to status termination

heuristics.rule: Negotiate the highest salary possible during STEM OPT — it directly affects H-1B lottery selection odds starting FY2027.
heuristics.condition: When a student on OPT or STEM OPT is evaluating a job offer and H-1B sponsorship is a future goal
heuristics.action: Prioritize compensation negotiation as a strategic immigration tool, not just a financial one
heuristics.because: Under the wage-based H-1B lottery system (FY2027+), higher wage level registrations are selected at higher rates, making salary a direct determinant of visa lottery success

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Only degrees whose CIP code appears on the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program list qualify for the 24-month STEM OPT extension; a non-qualifying degree means only 12 months of OPT total
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program list against your specific degree's CIP code via your university registrar or DSO before selecting a program or major

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Without E-Verify enrollment, an employer cannot legally hire a STEM OPT student; accepting an offer from a non-enrolled employer forces the student to choose between violating status or losing the job
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask HR directly before the offer stage; alternatively search the E-Verify employer search tool at uscis.gov

decision_factors.factor: OPT application timing relative to graduation
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Late applications cause gaps in work authorization that accumulate unemployment days and can jeopardize job offers; early applications maximize the buffer for USCIS processing delays
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Count back 90 days from your official program end date and mark that as the earliest filing date; build your graduation and job search timeline around this window

decision_factors.factor: Salary level relative to H-1B wage tiers
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Under the FY2027+ wage-based H-1B selection system, higher-wage registrations have statistically better lottery odds; a lower salary at a prestigious firm may yield worse lottery outcomes than a higher salary at a smaller firm
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research the prevailing wage levels for your occupation and location using the DOL Foreign Labor Certification Data Center; assess where a given offer falls within wage levels I–IV

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Pre-completion OPT hours count against the 12-month total; using pre-completion OPT reduces the post-graduation work buffer and can compress the timeline for finding H-1B-sponsoring employment
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate total pre-completion OPT hours used and subtract from the 12-month total to understand remaining post-completion eligibility

decision_factors.factor: Strength and clarity of Form I-983 Training Plan
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Vague or poorly written I-983s attract DHS audit scrutiny; a strong I-983 clearly connects job duties to the STEM degree and reduces audit risk
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review whether each job duty in the I-983 can be explicitly mapped to a specific course, skill, or competency from the STEM degree curriculum

decision_factors.factor: Cumulative unemployment days across both OPT and STEM OPT periods
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The 150-day limit during STEM OPT is cumulative across the entire extension period, not a fresh counter; students who had job gaps early in STEM OPT may hit the limit faster than they realize
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Maintain a running total from the start of the STEM OPT extension period; do not reset the counter when switching employers

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using pre-completion OPT for early work experience
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Every hour worked on pre-completion OPT is deducted from the 12-month post-completion OPT total, leaving less runway after graduation to find an H-1B-sponsoring employer
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in competitive fields where finding a sponsoring employer takes 6–12 months after graduation

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a job offer without verifying E-Verify enrollment for STEM OPT
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the employer is not enrolled, the student cannot legally start work on STEM OPT, creating an immediate gap that counts toward the 150-day unemployment limit while also putting them in a contractually awkward position
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who have already given notice to other opportunities or relocated for the role

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing a non-STEM degree program to graduate faster or for other academic reasons
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Forfeiting the 24-month STEM OPT extension means only 12 months to secure H-1B sponsorship — significantly compressing the timeline and increasing risk of status gaps
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who plan to stay in the US long-term but did not anticipate visa consequences when selecting their program

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Submitting a vague or generic Form I-983 to satisfy the employer quickly
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Increased probability of DHS audit; if the audit finds the I-983 insufficient, the student's STEM OPT authorization can be revoked retroactively, jeopardizing months of work history
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students working at employers unfamiliar with international student compliance who rely on HR to draft the I-983

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Prioritizing prestige of employer over salary level during OPT job search
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Under the FY2027+ wage-based H-1B lottery, a prestigious brand with a lower wage level may yield statistically worse H-1B selection odds than a less prominent firm offering higher compensation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in fields like consulting, research, or nonprofits where compensation is often below market and employer prestige is overweighted in decision-making

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on employer HR to manage OPT compliance reporting
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The legal obligation to report address changes, employer changes, and other status updates within 10 days rests with the student — not the employer; if HR misses a report, it is the student who loses status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students at large corporations with overburdened HR teams or at startups without dedicated immigration compliance staff

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying for OPT fewer than 90 days before graduation, resulting in a work authorization gap
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on academic completion and job searching, underestimating USCIS processing times and the complexity of the DSO recommendation step
failure_patterns.consequence: EAD card arrives after graduation and sometimes after a job start date, forcing the employer to delay onboarding or rescind the offer; every day without an EAD counts as an unemployment day
warning_signs: Student has not contacted DSO about OPT until after receiving a job offer
Student believes OPT starts automatically at graduation without a separate application

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 30-day I-765 filing window after DSO SEVIS recommendation, resulting in automatic denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not realize the 30-day clock starts from DSO's action in SEVIS, not from when they receive notification or gather documents
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT application is automatically denied; student must restart the process, losing weeks or months and potentially missing the post-completion filing deadline entirely
warning_signs: Student waiting to receive all supporting documents before initiating the I-765
Student unaware that DSO has already recommended OPT in SEVIS

failure_patterns.pattern: Exceeding the 90-day unemployment limit without realizing it, causing automatic F-1 status termination
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not track unemployment days systematically; they assume searching actively for work means they are not 'unemployed' in the immigration sense
failure_patterns.consequence: F-1 status is automatically terminated; the student is out of status and may be barred from re-entry or future visa applications
warning_signs: No personal log of unemployment days exists
Student has experienced multiple short job gaps between positions

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting STEM OPT work at a non-E-Verify employer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Student assumes E-Verify is a formality or that the employer will enroll once the student joins; employer HR is unaware of the requirement
failure_patterns.consequence: Work performed without valid STEM OPT authorization; student is technically out of status and the employer faces E-Verify compliance violations
warning_signs: HR has not mentioned E-Verify during onboarding
Employer is a small startup or sole proprietorship with no prior international hires

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting a vague Form I-983 that does not clearly connect job duties to the STEM degree
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students defer entirely to employer HR to draft the I-983; HR writes a generic job description rather than a degree-linked training narrative
failure_patterns.consequence: DHS audit finds the I-983 insufficient; STEM OPT authorization may be revoked and the student is left without valid work authorization mid-employment
warning_signs: I-983 uses phrases like 'general business skills' without naming specific courses or competencies
Supervisor has not read or meaningfully contributed to the I-983 content

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to report employer or address changes within 10 days, resulting in SEVIS violations
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume changes are administrative and low-priority; they are unaware the 10-day window is a legal compliance requirement, not a courtesy guideline
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS record is flagged for non-compliance; in serious cases DSO must terminate the SEVIS record, immediately ending F-1 status
warning_signs: Student changed jobs more than 10 days ago and has not contacted DSO
Student moved apartments and updated only the postal service, not SEVP Portal or DSO

diagnostic_questions.question: Has the student's DSO already recommended OPT in SEVIS, and if so, when?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the 30-day I-765 filing clock is already running and how much time remains before automatic denial
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Calculate exact days remaining in the 30-day window and prioritize immediate I-765 submission over all other application steps
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Determine how far from graduation the student is and whether the 90-day early filing window is open yet

diagnostic_questions.question: Does the student's degree CIP code appear on the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program list?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is eligible for the 24-month STEM OPT extension, which changes the entire timeline and strategy for transitioning to H-1B
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm E-Verify enrollment of any potential employers and begin STEM OPT extension application during the last 90 days of regular OPT
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Student has only 12 months of OPT; H-1B strategy must account for a much tighter window and higher urgency to secure sponsoring employer

diagnostic_questions.question: Is the employer the student is considering for STEM OPT enrolled in E-Verify?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the job offer is legally compatible with STEM OPT or will force an immediate status crisis upon onboarding
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed with I-983 Training Plan preparation and DSO notification
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do not accept or start the role; assess whether employer will enroll in E-Verify before the student's start date, and identify alternative offers

diagnostic_questions.question: How many unemployment days has the student accumulated since their OPT or STEM OPT period began?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is approaching or has exceeded the 90-day (OPT) or cumulative 150-day (STEM OPT) limit and whether F-1 status is currently at risk
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If approaching or over the limit, student must consult DSO immediately and assess whether a school transfer, reinstatement, or departure is necessary
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Establish a tracking system immediately if none exists; do not rely on memory or estimates

diagnostic_questions.question: Has the student used any pre-completion OPT hours, and if so how many?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: How much of the 12-month OPT total remains for post-completion use, directly affecting the runway available to secure H-1B sponsorship
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Calculate remaining post-completion OPT months and build the H-1B application timeline (especially lottery registration in March) around that expiration date
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Student has the full 12 months post-completion, providing maximum flexibility for job searching and employer evaluation

diagnostic_questions.question: Did the student change employers during STEM OPT, and if so, was a new Form I-983 submitted and the DSO notified within 10 days?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is currently in SEVIS compliance or has an unreported violation that could trigger status termination
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm I-983 is fully signed by both student and supervisor and that new employer's E-Verify enrollment has been verified
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If more than 10 days have passed since the employer change without reporting, student should contact DSO immediately to assess remediation options

industry_realities.common_belief: OPT starts automatically when you graduate and you can apply whenever you are ready after finishing your degree
industry_realities.actual_reality: OPT requires a proactive application with a strict timeline: the earliest you can apply is 90 days before graduation and the latest is 60 days after — missing these windows results in denial, not a delayed start
industry_realities.implication: Students must build OPT application planning into their graduation timeline, treating it as equally important as thesis submission or final exams

industry_realities.common_belief: As long as you are actively job searching, unemployment days do not count against your OPT limit
industry_realities.actual_reality: Unemployment days are counted purely by calendar days without valid employment — job searching activity has zero legal bearing on the count; only being employed in a qualifying position stops the clock
industry_realities.implication: Students must prioritize securing employment quickly after OPT start and treat any gap between jobs as a countdown, not a neutral period

industry_realities.common_belief: The STEM OPT 150-day unemployment limit is a fresh allowance on top of the regular OPT 90-day limit
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 150-day limit is cumulative across the entire STEM OPT extension period — it is not a per-job-gap allowance nor a reset from regular OPT; gaps early in the STEM OPT period permanently reduce the remaining buffer
industry_realities.implication: Students must track unemployment days from the very first day of STEM OPT and not assume they have three separate allowances across their OPT periods

industry_realities.common_belief: Any tech or engineering job qualifies for STEM OPT as long as the degree is in a STEM field
industry_realities.actual_reality: STEM OPT requires that the specific job duties be directly related to the specific degree — a computer science graduate in a general sales or management role without technical duties may not satisfy the requirement, even at a tech company
industry_realities.implication: Students must evaluate each job offer for degree-relatedness, not just employer industry, and ensure the I-983 can truthfully document that connection

industry_realities.common_belief: Cap-gap requires a separate filing or extension application to continue working after OPT expires
industry_realities.actual_reality: Cap-gap extension of both F-1 status and work authorization is automatic when an H-1B petition is timely filed — no separate USCIS filing is needed
industry_realities.implication: Students should focus energy on ensuring the employer files the H-1B petition on time rather than on preparing redundant paperwork; however they must confirm the petition has actually been filed

industry_realities.common_belief: Prestigious employers with strong brand names are the best choice during OPT because they improve H-1B prospects
industry_realities.actual_reality: Under the FY2027+ wage-based H-1B lottery system, salary level is a direct mechanical determinant of lottery selection odds — a higher salary at a less recognized employer may statistically outperform a lower salary at a brand-name firm
industry_realities.implication: Students should evaluate job offers through a dual lens of career development and compensation level relative to H-1B wage tiers, explicitly modeling lottery probability as part of the decision

industry_realities.common_belief: The employer or HR department is responsible for ensuring immigration compliance during OPT and STEM OPT
industry_realities.actual_reality: The legal obligation for SEVIS reporting, unemployment day tracking, and status compliance rests with the student — employer failures are the student's legal problem, not the employer's
industry_realities.implication: Students must treat themselves as their own immigration compliance officer, independently tracking all deadlines and reporting obligations regardless of what HR does or says

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Waits until receiving a job offer or until after graduation to ask DSO about OPT, assuming it can be sorted out quickly
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Contacts DSO exactly 90 days before graduation to initiate OPT regardless of employment status, understanding that USCIS processing takes months
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Late application creates EAD gaps that trigger unemployment day accumulation and can cause employers to rescind offers due to delayed start dates

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes HR or the school tracks unemployment days; does not maintain a personal log; learns about the 90-day limit only after approaching it
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Creates a spreadsheet on Day 1 of OPT tracking calendar days of employment and unemployment; sets an alert at 60 days to intensify job search if unemployed
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Automatic F-1 status termination upon exceeding the limit has no cure other than departure or reinstatement — prevention through tracking is the only viable strategy

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Employer evaluation for STEM OPT compatibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Evaluates employers on salary, role, and culture; asks about E-Verify only after receiving and accepting an offer, if at all
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats E-Verify enrollment as a first-pass filter before substantive interviews; removes non-E-Verify employers from consideration during STEM OPT periods
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Accepting a non-E-Verify employer offer during STEM OPT creates an immediate legal conflict that forces the student to choose between immigration status and the job

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Hands the I-983 entirely to HR and signs whatever is prepared; does not review whether job duties are explicitly linked to degree coursework
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Drafts the I-983 personally or collaborates closely with the supervisor; maps each job duty to a specific course, skill, or learning outcome from the STEM degree curriculum before HR review
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Vague I-983s attract DHS audits that can retroactively revoke STEM OPT authorization; a strong I-983 is the student's primary defense in an audit

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Negotiates salary purely on financial grounds; does not connect compensation level to H-1B lottery probability under the wage-based selection system
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Researches DOL prevailing wage levels for the target occupation and location; negotiates explicitly to reach a higher wage tier, understanding that each tier jump materially improves H-1B lottery odds
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Under the FY2027+ system, a wage tier difference can be the difference between H-1B selection and status expiration — salary negotiation is immigration strategy, not just compensation strategy

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Compliance reporting after employer or address changes
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Believes updating LinkedIn or informing the new employer's HR is sufficient; does not know the 10-day DSO reporting requirement exists
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats the 10-day reporting window as a hard deadline; reports changes via SEVP Portal and DSO notification before or on the date of the change when possible
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unreported changes are SEVIS violations that can result in status termination; the student bears the legal risk regardless of whether their employer or school reminded them

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Panics when OPT expiration approaches without H-1B approval; attempts to file extensions or asks employer to do something proactively to maintain status
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Understands cap-gap is automatic upon timely H-1B petition filing; focuses exclusively on confirming the petition was filed and monitoring approval rather than initiating unnecessary filings
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unnecessary filings waste money and can create conflicting records; understanding what is automatic versus what requires action prevents both panic and costly mistakes
